items.q: What is the difference between species richness and species evenness?
items.correct: Richness counts how many species exist; evenness measures how balanced their abundances are.
items.random_wrong: Richness is for plants, evenness for animals.
items.why: Students conflate these. A community with 10 species each having 100 individuals has high evenness; one with 10 species but 991 of species A and 1 of each other has low evenness despite identical richness.

items.q: How does the Brundtland Report define sustainable development?
items.correct: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as 'no growth'. The Brundtland definition explicitly balances development AND environmental concerns - it's about INTERGENERATIONAL equity, not freezing change.

items.q: Why is atmospheric nitrogen unavailable to most plants directly?
items.correct: N2 has a strong triple bond that most organisms cannot break.
items.misconception: Because plants cannot reach high enough to access it.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Despite 78% of air being N2, the molecule's triple bond is energetically costly to break. Only specialised nitrogen-fixing bacteria (and lightning, industrial Haber process) can convert it to usable forms.

items.q: Which body regulates wildlife trade internationally?
items.why: It's CITES. “IUCN” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Reintroducing wolves to Yellowstone is an example of?
items.why: Correct answer: Rewilding. “Captive breeding” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many national parks does the UK have (2024)?
items.why: Exact answer: 15 parks. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
